skill.name: plugin-settings
skill.description: Guides plugin developers through storing per-project Claude plugin settings in .claude/*.local.md files with YAML frontmatter and markdown context.
skill.summary: Build configurable Claude plugins with local YAML frontmatter settings.
skill.author: anthropics

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but most are false positives from markdown examples and expected bash helpers. No malicious intent, network exfiltration, or prompt injection attempt was found. The remaining risk is medium because the skill teaches hooks and commands to read local settings and optionally reuse markdown body text as model input.

medium_findings.title: Local Markdown Body Can Be Reused As Model Input
medium_findings.description: The skill documents patterns where the markdown body of a local settings file is extracted and fed back to Claude. This is an intended workflow, but the local file content must be treated as untrusted input to avoid prompt injection through project files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and semantically confirmed by examples that reuse markdown body content as a prompt. The risk is contextual because the file is local and user-managed, not remotely fetched.

low_findings.title: Shell Helpers Read User-Supplied Local File Paths
low_findings.description: The parser and validator scripts accept a path argument and read frontmatter from that file. This is normal local tooling, but callers should restrict paths to intended .claude settings files to avoid accidental disclosure in terminal output.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts clearly read caller-provided paths and may print parsed values. There is no network or privilege escalation path, so impact appears limited.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography hits occur in markdown frontmatter markers, descriptions, or file extension text. No cryptographic algorithm implementation or insecure hashing use was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations contain documentation text and markdown syntax, not crypto APIs. No evidence of DES, MD5, SHA1, or similar weak algorithm use was found.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Reconnaissance Alerts Are Deny-List Examples
low_findings.description: The /etc, /sys, .env, and secret path mentions are used in example validation logic that blocks sensitive paths. They do not read those locations or collect system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged paths appear inside checks that deny or validate input. There is no code that opens /etc, /sys, .env, or secret files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Substitution In Expected Bash Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Many static hits are shell command substitutions used to parse YAML frontmatter with sed, grep, awk, jq, and yq. They are expected for this skill, but users should quote variables and validate settings before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is real shell execution, but it is part of local parsing examples and helper scripts. No untrusted value is executed as a shell command in the reviewed snippets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Tmux Notification Target Comes From Local Settings
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code sends a notification to a tmux session named in local frontmatter. The value is quoted and not executed as shell code, but users should validate allowed session names in production hooks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The local setting influences the destination of keystrokes, which has operational risk. Arguments are quoted and no direct command injection evidence was found.

content.value_statement: Plugin developers need consistent per-project settings without committing user state. This skill shows how to use local markdown files with YAML frontmatter, validation, and defaults.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to read YAML frontmatter from hooks, commands, and agents.
Provides examples for enabled flags, modes, limits, and agent state.
Includes helper scripts for parsing and validating settings files.
Documents safe defaults, path validation, gitignore use, and restart requirements.
limitations: Settings changes require restarting Claude Code before hooks use them.
Shell examples parse simple YAML and are not a full YAML parser.
The pattern stores local project files only, not shared cloud configuration.
Users must avoid storing secrets or trusted instructions in settings files.

use_cases.title: Add project-specific plugin configuration
use_cases.description: Create local settings files that control plugin behavior without changing plugin source code.

use_cases.title: Standardize settings patterns across repositories
use_cases.description: Document one consistent format for plugin state, defaults, validation, and gitignore rules.

use_cases.title: Drive hook behavior from local settings
use_cases.description: Let hooks read enabled flags, validation modes, size limits, and agent coordination values.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic settings file
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a local settings file for my plugin with enabled, mode, and max_retries fields. Use safe defaults.

prompt_templates.title: Read plugin settings in a hook
prompt_templates.prompt: Show how my hook can read enabled and strict_mode from a local plugin settings file.

prompt_templates.title: Validate settings before use
prompt_templates.prompt: Add validation for mode, max_file_size, and optional file paths in my plugin settings pattern.

prompt_templates.title: Design an agent state workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a local settings and state pattern for coordinating multiple Claude Code agents across tasks.

output_examples.input: Create default settings for a validation plugin.
output: A local settings file is created under the project .claude directory.
The plugin is enabled with standard validation and conservative size limits.
The user is reminded to restart Claude Code before hooks read the new settings.

output_examples.input: Check whether strict mode is enabled for my plugin.
output: The settings file is found and parsed.
The enabled field is true, and strict mode is false.
The hook should continue with standard validation behavior.

output_examples.input: Review my settings pattern for safety.
output: The pattern uses a local file, quoted variables, and a quick exit when disabled.
It should add path allowlists before using file path settings.
It should never treat markdown body content as trusted instructions.

best_practices: Keep settings files local, gitignored, and scoped to one plugin.
Validate every parsed value before using it in hooks or commands.
Treat markdown body content as untrusted input, especially when reused as a prompt.
anti_patterns: Do not store secrets, tokens, or credentials in local settings files.
Do not execute values from YAML frontmatter as shell commands.
Do not assume missing or malformed settings are safe; use explicit defaults.

faq.question: What problem does this skill solve?
faq.answer: It gives plugin developers a repeatable local file pattern for per-project configuration and state.

faq.question: Where are settings stored?
faq.answer: Settings are stored in .claude/plugin-name.local.md files inside the project directory.

faq.question: Why use YAML frontmatter?
faq.answer: YAML frontmatter keeps structured settings separate from markdown notes or prompt context.

faq.question: Can settings change while Claude Code is running?
faq.answer: Hooks generally need a Claude Code restart before new settings are reliably used.

faq.question: Are secrets safe in these files?
faq.answer: No. Store secrets in approved secret managers or environment mechanisms, not local markdown settings.

faq.question: What should production hooks validate?
faq.answer: Validate booleans, modes, numeric ranges, file paths, and any text reused as model input.
